bullets.text: U.S.-Iran war escalation is driving risk-off sentiment; Trump says objectives will be met soon, but intelligence signals Iran skeptical of diplomacy.
Oil spikes on Iran conflict risk: Brent jumped ~5% after Trump vowed to hit Iran “extremely hard,” raising inflation and recession tail risks.
Equities wobble globally: U.S. futures sank and Asia markets reversed gains as investors repriced geopolitical risk and energy-driven margin/inflation pressure.
Trump floats leaving NATO, increasing alliance uncertainty and potentially shifting European defense burdens and procurement toward higher domestic spending.
Congressional Republicans struck a deal to end the DHS shutdown, reducing near-term travel/security disruption and removing a fiscal-policy overhang.
Supreme Court justices appear skeptical of limiting birthright citizenship; a loss could still normalize restrictive immigration messaging and policy experimentation.
SpaceX reportedly filed confidentially for an IPO, potentially a record listing and major liquidity event that could reset valuations across late-stage private markets.
Oracle’s large layoffs highlight ongoing cost-cutting even in “AI-driven” tech, signaling uneven labor demand and margin-defense priorities across software incumbents.
Intel shares jumped after buying back an Ireland fab stake, read as balance-sheet confidence and a potential inflection in the company’s turnaround narrative.
ADP reports 62,000 private-sector jobs added in March—better than expected but still subdued—supporting a “slower growth” view rather than reacceleration.
Memory prices are surging, squeezing hobbyist SBCs and broader hardware BOMs; sustained DRAM inflation could ripple into consumer electronics pricing and margins.
FDA approved Eli Lilly’s GLP-1 weight-loss pill, likely expanding access beyond injectables and intensifying competition, payer negotiations, and capacity/price dynamics.
Artemis II launched humans around the Moon; mission details underline schedule/engineering sensitivity (even small subsystems) but mark a major lunar program milestone.
Quantum research suggests fewer qubits may threaten widely used encryption; advances in quantum-safe cryptography and migration planning are becoming more urgent.
